Figure 1: Architecture of the RTT framework. The policy model first generates a response group for instructions. The top computes Â (i,t)

Table 1: Main results on instruction-following benchmarks. Fine-tuned method rows show scores with delta (↑ /↓ ) relative to the base model. Highlighted rows are our proposed RTT variants. Best results per model group are bolded and second-best are underlined. † We use discriminator initialized from Qwen3-4B-Base, ⋆ initialized from Llama3.2-3B-Base.
Table 4: Ablation on token weighting strategies for RL training.

Table 3: Ablation on group partitioning strategies across all model configurations. We compare Inter-sample vs. Intra-sample Token Group Normalization for advantage estimation. Best results per model are bolded.

Figure 2: Reward structure under standard GRPO and RTT-GRPO. In GRPO (left), each of G sampled completions receives one outcome-level reward, advantages are normalized along the sample axis only. The RTT-GRPO (right) assigns rewards over three dimensions, motivating two group normalization strategies, inter-sample and intra-sample group normalization.
Figure 4: Training dynamics of RTT-CSR vs. RL-CSR baseline. Left side shows model performance on IFEval, IFBench, and MulDimIF benchmarks, while right side shows response entropy.

Figure 5: Training stability comparison between RTT-CSR and RL-CSR on Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. We report rollout accuracy, entropy, KL loss, and clip fraction over training. RL-CSR exhibits severe instability, while RTT-CSR remains stable.
Table 8: Training configurations across methods and model backbones.
